original_abstract: In the Afrikaans-speaking South African theological tradition, Rudolf Bultmann’s work—specifically his program of demythologisation—has historically been met with significant skepticism and little positive reception. However, more than seventy years have passed since his seminal 1941 essay ignited one of the most intense theological debates of the twentieth century. While Bultmann’s legacy has receded into the background for many contemporary scholars, the 130th anniversary of his birth provides a timely opportunity to revisit his contributions. This paper aims to "dust off" Bultmann’s original 1941 thesis, seeking to listen to his voice directly, unmediated by the layers of critique and interpretation that have accumulated over the decades. Now that the fervor of the original debate has settled, a fresh engagement with his work suggests that Bultmann’s existential insights remain profoundly relevant within our current theological landscape.
humanized_abstract: It is common knowledge that Rudolf Bultmann’s demythologisation programme has met with virtually no approval in the Afikaans-based tradition of South African Dogmatics since the early 1950s. More than seventy years have since passed since Bultmann’s 1941 article on demythologisation provoked one of the greatest controversies of the twentieth century. Since his exegetical views have been largely overlooked in the theological discourse in the country in the meantime, a consideration of the life and work of this influential theologian should motivate us to a re-exploration of the question posed by him more than seven decades ago, the 130th anniversary of his birthday also serving as an impetus in this regard. As an opportunity to ‘get Bultmann’s proposal of 1941 out of the archives’ or as a call to rediscover the content of the thesis for our time, this article aims to breathe fresh air into the theologically somewhat ‘cool’ existential components of Bultmann’s demythologisation, and to endeavour to engage him more actively than ever before in the ongoing discussions in the dogmatic faculty.
